raw_case:
Atypical presentation of Wilms’ tumor in an adult: a case report and diagnostic considerations


Abstract

Wilms’ tumor (WT), while common in pediatric populations, is exceedingly rare in adults, accounting for only 0.5% of renal neoplasms. This report describes a 44-year-old patient diagnosed with stage II WT in the left kidney who underwent a radical nephrectomy, illustrating the diagnostic challenges encountered in adult cases. The patient’s symptoms and radiological findings were non-specific, complicating early detection. Histopathological analysis revealed a mixed-type tumor with a 60% blastemal component. Accurate histopathological evaluation is crucial for determining prognosis and guiding treatment. This case is rare due to its early-stage (stage II) diagnosis in an adult, which is almost unheard of in the literature, where most adult WT are advanced. This highlights the critical need for heightened clinical suspicion, early diagnostic efforts, and pivotal surgery-based treatment to improve outcomes in such rare cases.

Introduction

Wilms’ tumor (WT), also referred to as nephroblastoma is the most common primary malignant renal tumor in children, accounting for ~5%–6% of childhood neoplasms. However, it is extremely rare in adults, making up only 0.5% of all renal neoplasms [1]. The incidence in adults is exceptionally low, with fewer than 0.2 cases per million people annually. Because of its rarity, WT is rarely considered in older patients, often leading to delayed diagnosis and management, and it typically presents at a more advanced stage [2]. Clinically and radiologically, it is indistinguishable from renal cell carcinoma (RCC), making its diagnosis unexpected and often overdue. Additionally, the rarity of adult WT results in delayed application of appropriate and risk-adapted treatments based on international pediatric oncology protocols. This delay contributes to historically poorer survival outcomes in adults compared to children with Wilms’ tumor [3].

Case report

A 44-year-old patient presents to a urologist with gross hematuria and left flank pain that has been ongoing for the last 4 months. An ultrasound was followed by a thoraco-abdominopelvic computed tomography (CT) scan, which revealed a large renal mass centered on the middle and lower third of the left kidney, infiltrating the entire calyceal system up to the left ureter. Additionally, there is perirenal fat infiltration (Fig. 1). The patient underwent a radical nephrectomy, and the specimen was sent to the pathology laboratory for histopathological examination. Gross examination of the radical nephrectomy specimen revealed a polylobulated, firm, white yellow tumor appearing to infiltrate the renal sinus and hilum, reaching the renal capsule without perirenal fat infiltration (Fig. 2). No macroscopic tumor embolus was observed in the renal vein. Lymphadenectomy of the perirenal fat revealed a lymph node measuring 1.1 × 0.8 × 0.5 cm.Microscopic examination identified a triphasic WT with mixed components: 60% blastemal, 35% epithelial, and a very focal mesenchymal component. The tumor was classified as stage II according to the National Wilms’ Tumor Study (NWTS) Group staging system. There was no evidence of anaplasia or nephrogenic rests. The tumor infiltrated the renal sinus without lymph node metastasis. Immunohistochemical staining revealed positive expression for cytokeratin, WT1, and cyclin D1, while CD10, CK7, and AMARC were negative (Fig. 3).

Discussion

WT is an embryonal neoplasm that originates from nephrogenic blastemal cells and mimics the histology of the kidneys, often showing various patterns of differentiation. This disease affects ~1 in every 7000 children. It occurs equally in both genders and in both kidneys, with the average age of onset being 35 months for males and 45 months for females. Notably, 98% of cases are diagnosed in children under the age of 10. While WT has been reported in adults, it remains extremely rare [4]. The clinical presentation of WT in adults differs from that in children. In adults, the main symptom is often flank pain, accompanied by a history of weight loss and a sudden decline in performance status. Occasionally, the tumor may be asymptomatic and detected incidentally through CT scans and ultrasound. In contrast, children typically present with a painless, palpable abdominal mass. Metastatic disease at diagnosis is more common in adults, occurring in 30% of cases compared with 10% in the pediatric population. Staging criteria of WT are based on the anatomic extent of the tumor at nephrectomy piece. There are two main classifications: NWTS, which proposes a pre-chemotherapy nephrectomy; and the International Society of Pediatric Oncology (SIOP) classification which advises four to six cycles of chemotherapy previous to nephrectomy. Up to 50% of adult cases are diagnosed at an advanced stage (III–V) [5]. Whereas our current case is classified as stage II with no lymph node or distant metastasis.Kilton et al. [6] have established six strict diagnostic criteria for adult WT: it must be a primary renal tumor; it must have a primitive blastemal spindle or round cell component; it should form abortive or embryonal tubules or glomerular structures; the tumor must not show any areas diagnostic of RCC; there must be pictorial confirmation of histology; and the patient must be over 15 years old. Our case met all these criteria. Although rare, WT and RCC can occasionally be found simultaneously in the same kidney [1].There is no histopathologic difference between adult and child WT. WT consists of blastemal, stromal, and epithelial components, present in varying proportions that influence prognosis. A predominance of blastemal components is associated with poor outcomes despite therapy, while epithelial and stromal components represent intermediate risk tumors [7].Anaplasia, characterized by multipolar mitotic figures, enlarged nuclei at least three times the normal size, and hyperchromatic nuclei, is seen in ~10% of WT cases. Identifying anaplasia and describing its focal or diffuse distribution is crucial, as diffuse anaplasia is linked to higher relapse rates and worse outcomes [7].Immunohistochemistry analysis is typically not required, but markers such as cytokeratin, vimentin, desmin, actin, and WT1 can help distinguish predominant blastemal tumors from rare ones. WT1 is expressed in some blastemal and epithelial elements but not in the stromal components [8].Nephroblastoma is categorized into one of three risk groups based on histopathologic features, which is essential for determining appropriate therapeutic approaches. Blastemal-predominant WT are more aggressive and have a poorer prognosis, placing them in the high-risk group. Tumors with predominantly epithelial and stromal features are classified as intermediate risk. Tumors with a predominance of mesoblastic features fall into the low-risk group [7].The staging criteria for WT are determined by the anatomical extent of the tumor. There are two main staging systems: the prechemotherapy, surgery-based system developed by NWTSG and the postchemotherapy-based system developed by SIOP. The NWTSG and the Children’s Oncology Group recommend resecting the primary tumor before administering chemotherapy. In contrast, SIOP advocates for administering chemotherapy for four weeks prior to surgery [3].Although there are no established treatment guidelines for adult WT, radical nephrectomy followed by adjuvant chemotherapy, with or without radiotherapy, is recommended [9].

Conclusion

WT in adults is exceptionally rare and is often considered more aggressive than in children. However, recent evidence suggests that poorer outcomes may result from delayed diagnosis and lack of codified treatment protocols. This stage II case challenges the assumption of a poor prognosis in adults, emphasizing the importance of early detection, accurate staging, and timely surgical intervention. Such management may yield outcomes similar to pediatric cases, highlighting the need for increased clinical awareness and standardized treatment.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 44-year-old patient.
- **Chief Complaint:** Gross hematuria and left flank pain.
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptoms of gross hematuria and left flank pain lasting 4 months.
- **Past Medical History:** None provided.
- **Personal History:** None provided.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** None provided.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Thoraco-abdominopelvic CT revealed a large renal mass in the left kidney infiltrating the calyceal system and ureter, with perirenal fat infiltration.
  - Histopathology of radical nephrectomy showed triphasic Wilms’ tumor (60% blastemal, 35% epithelial, focal mesenchymal components) classified as stage II according to the NWTS staging system. No anaplasia, lymph node metastasis, or perirenal fat invasion was observed.
  - Immunohistochemistry: Positive for cytokeratin, WT1, and cyclin D1; negative for CD10, CK7, and AMARC.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - The main differential diagnosis for a renal mass in adults includes renal cell carcinoma (RCC), Wilms’ tumor (rare in adults), urothelial carcinoma, and other rare renal masses such as sarcoma.

2. **Evaluation of RCC:**
   - RCC was considered initially due to the patient’s age and non-specific imaging and clinical findings. However, histopathological examination did not show diagnostic features of RCC, such as clear cell or papillary histology, ruling it out.

3. **Evaluation of Wilms’ Tumor:**
   - Although uncommon in adults, Wilms’ tumor was considered based on histological findings of triphasic components (blastemal, epithelial, mesenchymal) and immunohistochemical positivity for WT1. The patient met diagnostic criteria for adult Wilms’ tumor, including the absence of RCC features and presence of embryonal patterns.

4. **Other Diagnoses Considered:**
   - Urothelial carcinoma was excluded due to the lack of transitional epithelial features and per the immunohistochemical profile. 
   - Sarcoma was ruled out given the lack of predominant stromal findings and negative staining markers.

5. **Final Diagnosis Determination:**
   - Adult Wilms’ tumor (stage II) was confirmed based on the histopathological and immunohistochemical findings, fulfilling all diagnostic criteria.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Stage II Wilms’ tumor (adult nephroblastoma) located in the left kidney. Diagnosis was substantiated by histopathologic and immunohistochemical findings, excluding other potential renal neoplasms.

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's chief complaints: gross hematuria and left flank pain for four months. These symptoms immediately make me think about possible renal or urinary tract issues. Gross hematuria can be caused by various conditions like kidney stones, infections, glomerulonephritis, or tumors. The flank pain points towards something affecting the kidney or ureter.

Next, the CT scan findings are crucial. The large renal mass in the left kidney infiltrating the calyceal system and ureter with perirenal fat infiltration suggests a malignant process. However, the histopathology from the nephrectomy says it's a triphasic Wilms’ tumor, stage II according to NWTS. Wait, the initial CT mentioned perirenal fat infiltration, but the histopathology report states no perirenal fat invasion. That discrepancy needs attention. Maybe the CT findings were overestimated, and the histopathology is more accurate since it's post-surgery.

Wilms’ tumor, or nephroblastoma, typically occurs in children, but this patient is 44. Adult Wilms’ is rare but possible. The triphasic histology (blastemal, epithelial, mesenchymal) is classic for Wilms’. The absence of anaplasia is good prognostically. Staging as II means the tumor is confined to the kidney but maybe extended into nearby structures, but here the histopathology says no perirenal fat invasion. NWTS stage II includes tumors that have been completely resected but may have local spread beyond the kidney, like into the renal sinus, but not into lymph nodes or distant sites.

The immunohistochemistry results: positive for WT1, which is a marker often associated with Wilms’ tumor. Cytokeratin positivity aligns with epithelial components. Cyclin D1 positivity might indicate cell cycle dysregulation. Negative for CD10 and CK7 could help rule out other renal tumors like renal cell carcinoma (RCC), which often express these markers. AMARC negative—not sure about that marker's significance, but maybe it's to exclude other sarcomas or mesonephric remnants.

Differential diagnosis considerations: In adults, renal cell carcinoma is more common, but the histopathology here points to Wilms’. Other possibilities like sarcomatoid RCC or other renal sarcomas could be considered, but the triphasic pattern and WT1 positivity strongly support Wilms’. The lack of anaplasia and lymph node involvement also supports a lower stage, which aligns with the given stage II.

So putting it all together: the patient's symptoms, imaging showing a mass, and the definitive histopathology and immunohistochemistry results confirm Wilms’ tumor. Even though adult cases are rare, the pathological findings are conclusive here. The staging as II based on NWTS criteria, despite initial CT findings, but the surgical pathology didn't find fat invasion, which would affect staging. Hence, the final diagnosis is Wilms’ tumor, stage II.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with gross hematuria and left flank pain, symptoms suggestive of a renal or urinary tract pathology. The chronicity (4 months) raises concern for a neoplastic process rather than acute causes like infection or nephrolithiasis.

<step 2> Thoraco-abdominopelvic CT findings of a large left renal mass infiltrating the calyceal system, ureter, and perirenal fat strongly indicate a malignant renal tumor. However, the histopathology after nephrectomy refines the diagnosis by demonstrating a triphasic Wilms’ tumor (blastemal, epithelial, mesenchymal components) without anaplasia, perirenal fat invasion, or lymph node metastasis. This aligns with NWTS stage II, which requires complete resection and localized tumor extension (e.g., renal sinus involvement) but excludes distant spread.

<step 3> Immunohistochemistry supports the diagnosis: WT1 positivity is characteristic of Wilms’ tumor, cytokeratin reactivity correlates with epithelial differentiation, and negative CD10/CK7 helps exclude renal cell carcinoma. The absence of anaplasia further confirms a favorable histology subtype.

<step 4> While Wilms’ tumor is rare in adults, the histomorphologic and immunohistochemical features are definitive. The discrepancy between CT-reported perirenal fat infiltration and histopathologic absence of fat invasion highlights the importance of surgical pathology for accurate staging.

### Answer:
Wilms’ tumor (nephroblastoma), stage II (NWTS), favorable histology.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with gross hematuria and left flank pain, symptoms suggestive of a renal or urinary tract pathology. The chronicity (4 months) raises concern for a neoplastic process rather than acute causes like infection or nephrolithiasis.

Thoraco-abdominopelvic CT findings of a large left renal mass infiltrating the calyceal system, ureter, and perirenal fat strongly indicate a malignant renal tumor. However, the histopathology after nephrectomy refines the diagnosis by demonstrating a triphasic Wilms’ tumor (blastemal, epithelial, mesenchymal components) without anaplasia, perirenal fat invasion, or lymph node metastasis. This aligns with NWTS stage II, which requires complete resection and localized tumor extension (e.g., renal sinus involvement) but excludes distant spread.

Immunohistochemistry supports the diagnosis: WT1 positivity is characteristic of Wilms’ tumor, cytokeratin reactivity correlates with epithelial differentiation, and negative CD10/CK7 helps exclude renal cell carcinoma. The absence of anaplasia further confirms a favorable histology subtype.

While Wilms’ tumor is rare in adults, the histomorphologic and immunohistochemical features are definitive. The discrepancy between CT-reported perirenal fat infiltration and histopathologic absence of fat invasion highlights the importance of surgical pathology for accurate staging.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: The main differential diagnoses for a renal mass in adults include renal cell carcinoma (RCC), Wilms’ tumor (rare in adults), urothelial carcinoma, and other rare renal masses such as sarcoma.
Step 2: Initial consideration of RCC was ruled out based on the absence of clear cell or papillary histology.

Step 3: Wilms’ tumor was considered due to the presence of triphasic components (blastemal, epithelial, mesenchymal) and immunohistochemical positivity for WT1, meeting the diagnostic criteria for adult Wilms’ tumor.
Step 4: Urothelial carcinoma was excluded due to the lack of transitional epithelial features and negative immunohistochemical profile.
Step 5: Sarcoma was ruled out given the absence of predominant stromal findings and negative staining markers.
Step 6: Adult Wilms’ tumor (stage II) was confirmed based on histopathological and immunohistochemical findings, excluding other potential renal neoplasms.
Final diagnosis: Stage II Wilms’ tumor (adult nephroblastoma) located in the left kidney.
